14.text: Standard math library, used here for the square root in attention scaling. JAX handles everything else.

15.text: Provides utilities for constructing file paths to locate the dataset on disk.

16.text: Python's built-in random module for shuffling the dataset into a random training order.

19.text: JAX's drop-in NumPy replacement that runs on GPU/TPU and supports automatic differentiation.

20.text: vmap is the star of this lab, it automatically vectorizes a single-example function across a batch. Write once, batch for free.

22.text: Fix the Python random seed so dataset shuffling is identical across runs, making results reproducible.

28.text: Path to the shared dataset file used consistently across all labs.

29.text: Download the names dataset from the internet if it is not already present locally.

32.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's repository.

34.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings, one per line.

35.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

38.text: The set of unique characters across all names forms our vocabulary. Each character gets a fixed integer ID.

39.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

40.text: The real vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 letters plus the BOS/EOS marker. PAD is tracked separately.

41.text: PAD token for aligning variable-length sequences in a batch.

42.text: The embedding table needs 28 rows: 27 real tokens plus one for PAD. But the output head only predicts over the 27 real tokens.

48.text: 4x larger embeddings, same scale-up as lab 04.

50.text: Two transformer layers give the model more depth to build progressively richer representations of character patterns.

51.text: Names up to 16 characters fit in the context window. Longer names get truncated, but most fit easily.

52.text: Each attention head operates on 16 dimensions, giving finer-grained representations than lab 05.

53.text: 32 sequences processed in parallel per training step. Batching amortizes overhead and gives the optimizer a smoother gradient signal.

54.text: Train for 1000 steps, same as all previous labs. But each step now processes 32 names at once, so the model sees 32x more data overall.

66.text: Pre-split enough random keys for all parameter initializations. JAX requires a fresh key for every random operation to stay deterministic.

67.text: Iterate through the pre-split random keys, giving each parameter its own unique source of randomness.

70.text: Token embeddings include a PAD row. Compare lab 04: vocab_size_with_pad for the embedding, vocab_size for the output head.

71.text: Position embeddings give the model a sense of order. Each position in the sequence has its own learned vector.

72.text: Output head, maps to real vocab only (27 tokens, no PAD).

75.text: Weight matrix that projects the input into query vectors for the attention mechanism.

76.text: Weight matrix that produces key vectors for positions to be matched against queries.

77.text: Weight matrix that produces value vectors carrying content to be mixed by attention.

78.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

79.text: First MLP weight matrix that expands the representation to 4x the embedding dimension for richer nonlinear transformations.

80.text: Second MLP weight matrix that compresses back to the original embedding dimension.

82.text: Sum up all array sizes in the parameter tree to report the total trainable parameter count.

90.text: Compute the mean of squared values along the last axis for RMS normalization.

91.text: Divide by the root-mean-square to normalize the input, keeping direction but stabilizing scale.

94.text: Forward pass for a SINGLE sequence. This is the key insight: you write the simplest possible code for one example, and vmap handles batching. Compare lab 04 where the entire forward pass was rewritten for (B, T, C) shapes.

101.text: Sequence length from the single example.

102.text: Token embeddings, single sequence, no batch dimension.

103.text: Position embeddings tell the model where each token sits. Without these, the model could not distinguish token order.

104.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer.

106.text: Iterate through each transformer layer. With two layers now, the model builds progressively richer representations.

107.text: Save the current hidden state for the residual connection after attention.

108.text: Normalize the input before passing it to the attention sub-layer for stable training.

110.text: Q projection, operates on (T, D), not (B, T, D). No batch handling needed in the model code.

111.text: Project the normalized input into key vectors that other positions will query against.

112.text: Project the normalized input into value vectors carrying content to aggregate.

114.text: Multi-head reshape, for a single sequence.

115.text: Reshape keys into separate attention heads, matching the same multi-head pattern as queries.

116.text: Reshape values into separate attention heads for parallel processing across heads.

118.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between all query-key pairs.

120.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask that blocks attention to future positions.

121.text: Set future positions to a large negative value so softmax gives them zero weight.

123.text: Padding mask: block attention to PAD positions. Even though this is single-example code, we still need the mask because padded tokens exist within each sequence.

124.text: Turn raw attention scores into a probability distribution so weights sum to one.

127.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors to produce the attention sub-layer output.

128.text: Transpose attention heads back and concatenate them into the full embedding dimension.

129.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output and add the residual connection in one expression. This completes the attention sub-layer.

132.text: Save the hidden state before the MLP for the second residual connection in this block.

133.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

134.text: Multiply by the expansion weights to widen the representation for richer transformations.

135.text: Apply ReLU activation to introduce nonlinearity between the two MLP linear layers.

136.text: Contract the MLP back to embedding dimension and add the residual. This completes the second sub-layer of the transformer block.

138.text: Final logits over the real vocabulary.

147.text: THE magic line. vmap(forward_single, in_axes=(None, 0, 0)) creates a batched version automatically. None means params are shared (not batched); 0 means batch along the first axis for inputs and masks. This one line replaces all of lab 04's manual batch reshaping.

150.text: Batched loss function. The forward pass is already batched thanks to vmap.

155.text: Call the vmap'd forward, returns (B, T, V) logits from (B, T) inputs.

156.text: Log-softmax is more numerically stable than computing softmax then taking the log separately, avoiding potential underflow issues.

159.text: Gather log-probs for target tokens using advanced indexing. Fancy indexing: for each batch and position, grab the log-prob of the correct target.

161.text: Zero out padding positions so they don't affect the loss.

162.text: Average only over real (non-padded) tokens. Dividing by sum(target_mask) ensures short sequences aren't penalized relative to long ones.

166.text: JIT-compile the gradient function. The grad + jit chain works the same as lab 05.

167.text: JIT-compile the loss function too, so we can cheaply evaluate the loss without computing gradients when we just need to monitor progress.

172.text: Same Adam hyperparameters as every other lab: lr=0.01, betas=(0.85, 0.99). Consistency makes it easy to compare across frameworks.

173.text: First moment state, zeros matching param shapes.

174.text: Second moment state tracks the magnitude of recent gradients per parameter, enabling Adam's adaptive learning rate behavior.

178.text: Allocate fresh output dictionaries rather than mutating old ones. Immutability is essential for JAX's functional transformations to work correctly.

180.text: Update the running mean of gradients (first moment). This smooths out noisy single-step gradients, acting like momentum.

181.text: Update the running mean of squared gradients (second moment). This tracks gradient scale so rarely-updated parameters get larger steps.

182.text: Correct the first moment estimate for its bias toward zero in early training steps.

183.text: Correct the second moment estimate for its initialization bias in early steps.

184.text: Compute the updated parameter value using the bias-corrected moments and learning rate.

191.text: Batch construction, same padding strategy as lab 04, but using JAX arrays instead of PyTorch tensors.

193.text: Gather batch_size documents from the dataset, cycling through with modular indexing to cover the full training set.

195.text: Convert each document's characters into integer token IDs that the model can process.

196.text: Wrap each name with beginning-of-sequence tokens to mark the start and end boundaries.

197.text: Truncate to the maximum context length. Most names are short enough, but this guard prevents overflow.

200.text: Pad to the longest sequence in the batch.

201.text: For each sequence, create aligned input tokens, target tokens, padding masks, and target masks, all padded to the same length.

203.text: The actual number of real tokens in this sequence (before padding). Used to know where real data ends and padding begins.

205.text: Targets: shifted tokens + 0 as dummy for masked positions.

207.text: Target mask: 1.0 for real positions, 0.0 for padding. Used to weight the loss.

213.text: Pack all input sequences into a single JAX array of shape (B, max_T) for efficient batched computation.

214.text: Pack target tokens into a matching JAX array. Padded positions use dummy values that the target mask will zero out.

215.text: Boolean mask that tells attention which positions are padding and should be ignored.

216.text: Float mask that weights the loss so padding positions contribute zero to the gradient.

224.text: Iterate over training steps, processing a batch of 32 names per step.

225.text: Assemble a padded batch of tokenized sequences with all necessary masks, ready to feed into the vmap'd forward pass.

227.text: Evaluate the cross-entropy loss on this batch using the JIT-compiled loss function.

228.text: Compute gradients for all parameters using the JIT-compiled gradient function.

230.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

231.text: Functional Adam update, replace the entire state.

233.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

239.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors high-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

241.text: Initialize a PRNG key for sampling during generation. Each sampling step will split off a fresh sub-key to stay deterministic.

242.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

243.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

244.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the maximum sequence length. Each step feeds all generated tokens through the model.

246.text: No padding during single-sequence inference, all False mask.

247.text: Use forward_single directly, no need for vmap when generating one sequence at a time.

248.text: Grab the logits for the last generated position and divide by temperature. Lower temperature makes the distribution sharper.

249.text: Split PRNG key for this sampling step.

250.text: Draw one token from the probability distribution to continue generating the name.

251.text: When the model predicts BOS, it signals the name is complete, so we stop generating.

253.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence for the next forward pass.

254.text: Map each token ID back to its character, skipping the leading BOS, to produce the generated name as a readable string.

255.text: Print the finished name. These names were invented by the model from statistical patterns it learned during training.
